mnemonics.title: SPAN Properties
mnemonics.content: SPAN: S-ubspace (always), P-reserves inclusion (if S⊆T then span(S)⊆span(T)), A-ccessible (contains original vectors), N-o decrease (adding vectors never reduces span)

mnemonics.title: Span Visual
mnemonics.content: Think of SPAN as SPAGHETTI - a single vector stretches into a line (like spaghetti), two can stretch into a plane (like a flat sheet), three fill the entire space!

mnemonics.title: Basis Check
mnemonics.content: To check if spanning set is BASIS: Must be B-oth S-panning (covers all) and I-ndependent (no redundancy)

acronyms.expansion: Linear Combination Sum - a₁v₁ + a₂v₂ + ... + aₖvₖ
acronyms.usage: Definition of any vector in span(S)

acronyms.expansion: Spanning Set - set whose span equals the whole space
acronyms.usage: Describing a set that generates all vectors in V

visualTips.visualization: Imagine an elastic string anchored at origin, stretched in the direction of the vector - it creates a line through origin
Imagine two strings anchored at origin, forming a tent. The fabric between them creates a flat plane through origin
Two vectors pointing in same or opposite direction - like one string, no new direction added
All spans form 'closed' regions that include origin and are 'flat' (no curves) - they are linear subspaces

analogies.analogy: Span is like a recipe - given a set of ingredients (vectors), span tells you all possible dishes (linear combinations) you can cook.
A spanning set is like alphabet letters - with them, you can spell any word (vector) in the language (vector space).
A spanning set is like having all pieces of a puzzle (can make the picture), while a basis is like having exactly the right pieces (no waste, all necessary).
Adding vectors to a span is like adding more tools to your toolbox - you can build at least as much as before, possibly more.
